# Bench harness

`bench/bench.go` is a stand-alone Go program (not part of `go test`) that
seeds a synthetic corpus, runs a fixed query workload, and writes a
timestamped JSON artefact to `bench/results/`.

## Usage

```sh
# Bring up Postgres + the embed sidecar first.
make up
make migrate

# Default: 50k docs, 1k queries.
go run ./bench

# Or knob it down.
go run ./bench -docs 5000 -queries 200

# CI smoke target.
go run ./bench -docs 1000 -queries 100 -smoke
```

## Methodology

* Documents are generated by `internal/seed.Generator` with a fixed seed
  (default 42). Each document is 200–2000 characters drawn from a
  topic-specific vocabulary, so vector retrieval has signal on top of
  the keyword path.
* Indexing runs through the same `UpsertDoc` path the HTTP server uses,
  with `-workers` concurrent goroutines and `-batch` items per embed
  call. Idempotent re-runs are cheap because content hashes are unique.
* The query workload is a different seed from the corpus to avoid trivial
  cache hits. Each mode (`keyword`, `vector`, `hybrid`) runs the full
  query list back-to-back; latency is the wall time of the API-level
  `engine.Query` call, not the underlying SQL alone.
* Percentiles are nearest-rank: `p[k] = sorted[ceil(p * N) - 1]`.

## What the numbers don't capture

* Network latency between client and server. Bench is in-process.
* GC pauses on a fully loaded host — each run is a clean process so
  earlier work can't pollute the heap.
* Cold-start: the HNSW index is warm by the time queries begin.

If you intend to compare runs, fix `-seed`, `-docs`, and `-queries`
across both runs and don't restart the database between them.

## Output

Each invocation writes `bench-YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS.json`. Schema:

```json
{
  "started_at": "...",
  "completed_at": "...",
  "docs": 50000,
  "queries": 1000,
  "embedder": "...",
  "index_docs_per_sec": 412.3,
  "index_chunks_per_sec": 1850.7,
  "modes": [
    {"mode": "keyword", "n": 1000, "p50_ms": 4.2, "p95_ms": 11.8, ...}
  ]
}
```
